# Demonstration: Static Analysis vs Dynamic Execution Gap

# This scripttest executes several programs
exec echo "Hello from scripttest"
stdout 'Hello'

exec mcpdiff --help
stderr 'Usage'

# Create a Go program that analyzes itself
mkdir -p demo

# Create analyzer.go using setstdin and cat
setstdin package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "go/ast"
    "go/parser"
    "go/token"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Static analysis sees function calls...")
    src := `package main
import "os/exec"
func runTest() {
    cmd := exec.Command("mcpdiff", "--help")
    cmd.Run()
}`
    
    // Parse the source
    fset := token.NewFileSet()
    node, _ := parser.ParseFile(fset, "", src, 0)
    
    // Find exec.Command calls
    ast.Inspect(node, func(n ast.Node) bool {
        if call, ok := n.(*ast.CallExpr); ok {
            if sel, ok := call.Fun.(*ast.SelectorExpr); ok {
                if sel.Sel.Name == "Command" {
                    fmt.Println("Found exec.Command call")
                    // But we can't determine at compile time what it will execute!
                    fmt.Println("Cannot determine target binary statically")
                }
            }
        }
        return true
    })
}
exec cat > demo/analyzer.go

exec go run demo/analyzer.go
stdout 'Found exec.Command'
stdout 'Cannot determine target binary statically'

# This demonstrates the fundamental limitation:
# 1. This scripttest executes echo and mcpdiff
# 2. Static analysis of this test would only see "exec" commands
# 3. It can't determine which specific binaries are executed
# 4. Traditional callgraph tools miss these dynamic connections
